title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita

Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between energy intensity and GDP per capita for various countries in 2022. The vertical axis measures energy intensity in kilowatt-hours per dollar, ranging from 0 kWh to over 4 kWh. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from $1,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by continent: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). Several countries stand out with high energy intensity values above 4 kWh per dollar, including Turkmenistan, North Korea, Venezuela, and Bahrain. These nations are distributed across different GDP levels, from roughly $2,000 to over $50,000 per capita. In the mid-range of energy intensity, between 1 and 3 kWh, countries like Libya, Russia, Iran, Canada, and Kuwait appear. Many African nations, such as Burundi, Chad, Cameroon, and Mozambique, cluster in the lower-left portion of the chart, showing both low GDP per capita (under $5,000) and low energy intensity (mostly under 1 kWh). European countries like Denmark, Norway, and the United Kingdom generally occupy the right side of the chart with higher GDP per capita and moderate energy intensity around 1 kWh. Asian countries show a wide spread, from low-income, low-intensity nations to high-income, high-intensity ones like Qatar and Saudi Arabia. The data reveals no simple linear relationship, with high energy intensity occurring at various income levels.
